title: 'Becoming more plausible': Recession warning if Iran war rages on
text:
T﻿here is a strong possibility Australia could be headed for a recession if the war against Iran spills into the second half of 2026, economists have warned.

Fears of a recession loom large as the battle for control over the Strait of Hormuz enters a dramatic new phase and dashes any hope of a swift end to the conflict in the Middle East.

﻿If a peace agreement is reached by the end of June , economic growth in Australia would still be slashed by more than half to 0.7 per cent, pushing unemployment to over five per cent for the first time since 2021, according to Deloitte modelling conducted for the Australian Financial Review.

LIVE UPDATES: Peace plans shattered as Trump weighs more strikes

Fears of a recession loom large in Australia if the war against Iran lasts beyond June. (Graphic: Tara Blancato)

Any longer than that, unemployment could teeter dangerously close to above six per cent, driving Australia to its first recession since the COVID-19 pandemic.

"The longer this conflict drags on, the worse it looks, including for Australia," AFR's Economic Editor John Kehoe told Today .

"If it drags onto mid-year, the Strait of Hormuz doesn't get open and the oil doesn't start flowing through, there is a real possibility of a recession in Australia."

Kehoe said this worst-case scenario is edging closer to reality as US President Donald Trump struggles to find a solution to Iran's blockade of the critical waterway.

He warned﻿ a recession in Australia becomes more likely every week that commercial vessels are locked out of the Strait.

A prolonged war would spell bad news for the global economy, as well as local job and inflation data.﻿

Unemployment could teeter dangerously close to above six per cent, driving Australia to its first recession in six years. (Janie Barrett)

"Becoming more plausible because we're not seeing a lot of good news coming out of the Middle East," Kehoe added.

"We'd want to see something in terms of the Strait of Hormuz opening within the next few weeks.

"If it drags on much beyond that, you'd have to say the economic outcomes in Australia are not going to be good."

While Treasurer Jim Chalmers has flatly denied any suggestion of a recession, the RBA has flagged that a domestic recession could be a necessary evil to drive inflation down.

"We don't want to have a recession, but if it's hard to get inflation down, then we're going to have to deal with that, possibly," RBA governor Michele Bullock said.

READ MORE: Young woman drowns at famous Aussie swimming hole

Treasurer Jim Chalmers has previously ruled out that the government was preparing for a recession. (Alex Ellinghausen)

Chalmers said over the weekend that the Australian economy was weathering the Middle East fallout "from a position of relative strength"﻿.

"[We] know the effects of this crisis will be felt for a long time and as I've said, we are considering that as we prepare the budget," Chalmers said.

title: Three in four Aussie workers skip this simple check - and it could cost them thousands
text:
Three in four Aussies aren't regularly checking their payslips, new research shows, even as pay mistakes continue to crop up across the workforce.

Fresh data from accounting software company Reckon points to a disconnect between the amount of trust people place in their pay and how often errors actually occur.

Being paid the wrong amount remains the most common issue, with 46.7 per cent of workers reporting they've been affected - typically due to incorrect hours, rates or salaries.

Almost half of all Aussie employees surveyed noticed they had been paid a "wrong amount". (A Current Affair)

Casual employees were the most likely to spot discrepancies, likely because their hours fluctuate and changes are easier to spot.

Missing overtime or penalty rates followed, flagged by 31.1 per cent of workers, with full-time employees most likely to notice when those entitlements weren't included.

Incorrect leave balances round out the top three, affecting 18.6 per cent of workers, with errors in annual and sick leave often going unnoticed until they accumulate.

The findings are based on a survey of 1,000 workers, along with data from the Fair Work Ombudsman and the ATO. (Getty)

Even with those figures, many Australians still aren't checking closely.

Almost half of those surveyed said they don't review their payslips because they trust their employer or payroll system, while others assume it's correct because they've never noticed a problem before.

A further 23.2 per cent say they only look at the amount deposited into their bank account, rather than the breakdown behind it – suggesting a large share of workers may not be reviewing their payslips at all.

title: This photo looks harmless. It's actually the hook in a costly 'reverse scam'
text:
E﻿xclusive: It looks like a normal photo of damaged makeup but alarm bells went off in Tanielle Jai's mind as soon as she saw it.

The Queensland-based makeup artist and business owner has recently seen a spike in customers trying to use AI to 'reverse scam' her business.

It's forced her to ﻿overhaul her returns process, which is how she caught the photo.

A photo of a cream bronzer which Tanielle Jai believes to have been AI-altered. (Supplied)

The customer who sent it claimed it showed that the bronzer they ordered had been damaged in transit and requested a full refund.

But Jai noticed some inconsistencies, like the cream product appearing to be runny and crumbly at the same time.﻿

﻿"The formula we have just couldn't physically do that," Jai told nine.com.au.

"We also ran the image through AI detection websites and it came back as completely fake."

Have you got a story? Contact reporter Maddison Leach at mleach@nine.com.au

She asked the customer for a video of the damaged makeup, which they sent.

But Jai said there were inconsistencies with that too, leading her to believe both the photo and video had been altered using AI to fraudulently claim a replacement or refund.

A screenshot from the video the customer sent, which Jai also suspected was AI-altered. (Supplied)

﻿It's become a surprisingly common occurrence for Australian small business owners.

Several have reported a surge in AI 'reverse scams' wherein consumers claim a product arrived damaged and provide AI-altered photos as proof.

They then request a refund or replacement, effectively cheating businesses out of cash or stock.

﻿"Over the last two months we have seen an increase in attempts to use AI to claim faulty products," Jai said.

Just last week a customer from the US emailed her claiming she didn't like the items she ordered for her own makeup kit ﻿and that they arrived damaged.

Jai requested photos of the damage, but the images the customer sent appeared to have been "tampered with".

She said AI detection software also flagged them as having been altered.

﻿Jai went back to the customer with her findings and suggested a video call to prove the products had actually been damaged.

That's when the customer's story allegedly changed.

﻿"Straight away she goes, 'actually I got this for a client, my client edited [the photos],'" Jai said.

"I actually got annoyed, and I just said, 'with all due respect ... why would you do this to a small business? It's stealing.'"

﻿But Jai and her two staff can't catch every dodgy photo and she has no doubt some customers have gotten away with it in the past.

And if they're willing to do it to her, an established makeup artist with nearly half a million Instagram followers, Jai's certain they're willing to do it to smaller brands.

Jeannie Paterson, Professor of Law at The University of Melbourne, previously confirmed to nine.com.au that this kind of consumer fraud is a criminal offence.

"Fraud means intentionally deceive someone and you cannot argue it's not intentional. You're trying to operate a fraud on a business," she said.

"We're in this spiral of fakes and frauds. I just feel awful about it."

She suspects some businesses will raise prices to cover the cost of additional shipping insurance or losses due to fraud.

Many are likely to start requiring video proof of faulty or damaged goods.

﻿Jai has taken things one step further.

Tanielle Jia is a prolific Australian makeup artist and small business owner. (Instagram/@taniellejaimua)

She now runs all photos and videos of damaged products through AI detection software and checks metadata for red flags.﻿

She's also started asking customers who claim their products arrived broken ﻿to video call her and show the damage in real time.

"It's is terrifying that the only solution that I can see is that we have to do live calls with these customers to verify."

title: Farmer braces for backlash after speaking up in support of transmission lines
text:
Farmer Craig McIntosh owns 4,000 hectares at Tragowel, near Kerang in Victoria's north and has been a vocal supporter of the controversial VNI West project.

VNI West is a proposed 500-kilometre high voltage project that will run through farmland to connect renewable energy zones in Victoria and New South Wales.

Not only that, Mr McIntosh has offered up his property for the transmission lines.

"Others didn't want it, so me and a couple of other neighbours got together and said, 'Why not bring it through our farms?'," Mr McIntosh said.

One reason he supports it is that he has farmed all his life around a transmission line that was built about 60 years ago, bringing power to a region that previously relied on generators.

"So it's been 60 years and I'm thinking we've got to do something to catch up and if we don't do something it could be another 60 years and we [will say] we should have built it 60 years ago," he said.

"And you can farm under it — I've never known anyone to crash into [a tower] or stop farming around them."

Survey access has been provided for approximately 40 per cent of the preferred easement, with the owners of about 170 properties across the broader project area agreeing to have surveyors on their land.

'No government should be doing that'

Many farmers have been vehement in their opposition to the transmission lines.

Victorian Farmers Federation (VFF) president Brett Hosking's property is within the proposed easement.

Brett Hosking says the VNI west project has been stressful for farmers. (Supplied: Victorian Farmers Federation)

"The stress it's placed on me with VNI West coming through and the poor engagement … in fact, the lack of engagement … from VicGrid," Mr Hosking said.

"The almost removal of my rights to even have any input over what's going to be placed on my land without consent.

"It's horrific, and no government should be doing that."

As a previous board member of Farmers for Climate Action, Mr Hosking said it was not a matter of being against new electricity infrastructure in general.

"Agriculture is doing the heavy lifting when it comes to reducing emissions, yet we seem to still be bearing the brunt of every other sector of the economy [that] needs to reduce their own emissions," he said.

Diversifying income

By hosting 10km of transmission line, Mr McIntosh would receive more than $2 million in state government compensation over the next 25 years.

He said that would diversify his income streams, and help drought-proof his property.

"Especially at the moment with rising fuel and fertiliser costs, I'm a big believer that we need different revenue streams to be profitable," Mr McIntosh said.

It would also enable a proposed wind turbine facility on his property to proceed, generating extra income.

A map showing the VNI West route. (Supplied: VicGrid)

"We've got a project going at the moment with wind turbines and we're pushing that quite hard, so there's a little bit of self-interest there, because we need the powerline to plug into the wind turbines anyway," he said.

While Mr McIntosh expected backlash for speaking publicly in favour of VNI West, he said he was not concerned.

"I've always been up-front with what I'm doing and how I'm doing it," he said.

'Needless and wasteful'

The Victoria Energy Policy Centre's Professor Bruce Mountain has been pushing for an alternative plan for transmission in Victoria.

He previously criticised the bill of the project, estimating it could cost up to $28 billion over the next decade and said there were cheaper options to expand transmission capacity in Victoria.

Bruce Mountain has criticised the VNI West project. (ABC News: Peter Garnish)

"All of our research shows it undermines the energy transition — it's needless, it imposes enormous cost on consumers," Professor Mountain said.

" It is a major policy failure. "

In 2023, Professor Mountain presented a "Plan B" involving using existing Victorian grid upgrades and a higher voltage.

Project will continue

VicGrid chief executive Alistair Parker said while the goal was for voluntary access, the project would continue regardless.

"Our strong preference is to work with landholders and agree voluntary access for the work required to plan the project," Mr Parker said.

In November, VicGrid employees were blocked in their attempt to gain entry to some properties. (ABC Rural: Angus Verley)

" We will continue to engage respectfully and seek voluntary agreement, but all Victorians are relying on us to deliver this project to keep the lights on. "

Notices of proposed entry have been issued to 26 properties where voluntary access for ecology surveys has not been agreed.

If access is denied, authorised officers may issue warnings, directions or infringement notices for refusing, obstructing or unreasonably delaying entry, which may carry a fine of about $800.

If voluntary access cannot be agreed and access continues to be blocked, VicGrid can also apply for a court order for access.

According to VicGrid, refusing access does not affect whether the project proceeds, but without access, there may be gaps in information where assumptions need to be made.

title: Loved ones fight for change after melanoma takes another young life
text:
In July last year, 29-year-old Emily Robertson-Miller, was at work when she fell ill.

About 30 hours later, she lost her life.

A melanoma, a deadly form of skin cancer, had metastasised and spread to her brain, something the young woman was unaware of.

On Sunday in Geraldton, in Midwest Western Australia, they marched for her legacy.

A sudden loss

At the melanoma march, Emily's loved ones were still feeling the heavy grief of losing her so unexpectedly, less than a year ago.

It was a shock to everyone who knew her.

"We didn't know that she was even unwell," Emily's partner Ruby Blakeway said.

A melanoma had been removed from Emily's shoulder in 2022 and she had seemingly recovered.

Friends describe Emily Robertson-Miller as a passionate advocate for others. (Supplied: Ruby Blakeway)

Ms Blakeway said when Emily fell ill at work, they first thought it was a stroke.

The mental health support worker was rushed to hospital, but when her condition deteriorated, she was put on life support and flown to Perth for surgery.

During surgery the doctors found two large tumours in Emily's brain, which had caused unsurvivable damage.

"She'd been having some headaches and mood changes, but she had a history of quite bad migraines," Ms Blakeway said.

"No-one had even considered that the melanoma might have spread to her brain.

"Even though she and the doctors did everything right, it was an aggressive kind that slipped through."

According to the Melanoma Institute, melanoma is the deadliest form of skin cancer, and the most common cancer affecting people in their 20s and 30s.

Australia has the highest rates in the world, with one in every 18 Australians developing it.

Money for research

Emily's friends and family say their loss "lit a fire", helping them raise thousands of dollars for Geraldton's 15th Melanoma March on Sunday.

In recent years, the yearly walk has been held in honour of another Geraldton local, Jason O'Brien, who lost his life to melanoma in 2021, at age 47.

Ms Blakeway said the money would assist research to save others.

"Hopefully long term we can find medical interventions to be able to catch this early," she said.

She also wanted more people to be aware of the dangers of skin cancer.

"Em was a goth, she didn't do the 'sun thing' very often," she said.

"It's still so important to be sun aware, get your moles checked, even if you're not sure."

Emily's friend, Casey Clark, said some people who were highly sensitive found sunscreen too overwhelming to wear, but there were many options for people who struggle with this.

"There are so many sunscreens out there now that dry matt, don't smell like sunscreen but are still SPF 50," Ms Clark said.

"Find the sunscreen you like, that doesn't overwhelm you or make you uncomfortable to wear and reapply."

Emily Robertson-Miller (left) with her partner Ruby Blakeway. (Supplied: Ruby Blakeway)

Emily 'wanted to do a lot of good'

Emily's family and friends say she was a mental health support worker and a passionate advocate within the local diverse and LGBTQIA+ community.

Before she passed, Emily had been talking about starting a community food cupboard for people in need, improving blood donation facilities and starting a soup kitchen.

"She wanted to do a lot of good like that," Ms Clark said.

Ms Blakeway said talking about her loss was hard, but it felt good to do something in Emily's name.

"I like to think she'd be proud of us," she said.

title: Commuters stuck waiting for Brisbane rail buses
text:
Commuters in Brisbane have vented their frustration after experiencing long waits for bus replacement services at a number of train stations.

There have been numerous track closures across Brisbane's rail network since April 10 due to a mix of major track works and ongoing industrial disputes.

On Monday buses replaced trains between Northgate and Bowen Hills, north of the city, and between Boggo Road and the Gold Coast's Varsity Lakes.

Joanne McCarthy said her commute from Caboolture to Roma Street, which normally takes one hour, took two-and-a-half.

She said passengers were made to get off the train at Geebung and join what was already a line of hundreds spanning across the rail bridge just before 7am.

"There were no buses there waiting for us," she said.

Loading...

She said she waited 45 minutes before she was able to get on a bus in the hot sun.

"We had no communication whatsoever about what was happening.

"I was thinking about jumping in an Uber, but you couldn't even get to the front of the line to get down the stairs to get an Uber."

What you need to know as Qld track closures extended After a week of "chaotic" disruptions, passengers should prepare for further impacts with the extended April track closures to get into full swing.

Another commuter told the ABC they waited 40 minutes at Eagle Junction station, with long queues still behind them.

"While waiting for a bus to Central, two buses to Helensvale stopped. About three people got on while hundreds continued to wait to get to work in the city," they said.

The Nudgee resident said it took 90 minutes to get to work instead of the usual 35.

Another commuter said Geebung station did not have enough replacement buses going to the city.

"Geebung had at least 100 people waiting for the bus to the CBD," they said.

South East Queensland Transport Association founder Imogen Buckley said that while there had been similar track closures in previous years that needed replacement buses, the disruptions were exacerbated by the fuel crisis.

"The fact that there are commuters waiting on the platform for a line that stretches outside of the station, it's embarrassing; it makes public transport look terrible," she said.

She said more frequent rail buses were needed, adding she believed the track closures were a "necessary evil."

"We need better infrastructure so we can have less track closures in the future and have a frequent and reliable network," she said.

There are multiple future track closures planned for April.

The transport minister and the Department of Transport and Main Roads have been contacted for comment.

title: Community seeks to become sole managers of NSW national park in two years
text:
As the community gathered to celebrate 30 years since Booderee National Park in Jervis Bay was handed back to the traditional owners, the crowd heard momentous news.

In two years, the Wreck Bay Aboriginal Community Council plans to take on sole management of the park at Wreck Bay, independent of Parks Australia.

Booderee National Park in New South Wales, Uluru-Kata Tjuta National Park and Kakadu in the Northern Territory are the only three Aboriginal-owned and jointly managed Commonwealth parks in Australia.

Joint management is when the traditional owners and Parks Australia work together to care for country.

Last year, Uluru celebrated 40 years, Kakadu is in its 43rd year of joint management.

Beverley Ardler says Wreck Bay Aboriginal Community Council will take over in May 2028. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

"We refer to ourselves as brother and sister parks," chair of the Joint Board for Booderee, Beverley Ardler said.

She was speaking at the Booderee handback 30th Anniversary Celebrations at Green Patch in Jervis Bay on Saturday, where she also confirmed that in May 2028, the park will become solely managed by the Wreck Bay Aboriginal Community Council (WBACC), without Parks Australia's input.

They would be the first of the three jointly managed parks to do so.

Representatives from Kakadu National Park were also at the celebrations. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

"We as Wreck Bay people are going, enough's enough, we're not waiting another 60 years, we're taking it right here, right now," she said.

"In two years, our people will be running and managing this park."

Guests included representatives from Uluru- Kata Tjuta National Park. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

Ms Adler, who is also a member of the WBACC, said all three jointly managed parks had 99-year lease agreements with the government, originally agreed to allow time to prepare and transition to sole management.

"But we've progressed as parks and we built the capacity within our own community ... to manage the rest of our land, not jointly but solely," she said.

Parks Australia said in a statement they were now working with the community to develop a pathway towards sole management.

Park manager George Brown is a member of the Wreck Bay community. As a 12-year-old school boy, he was a witness to the handover of ownership in 1995.

At the celebrations, he paid tribute to the work of elders then and now.

The 30th anniversary celebrations were held under the trees in Booderee National Park. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

"The return of the land in the 90s, the establishment of joint management and the continued strength of your culture and our culture did not happen easily," he said.

" They happened because you stood firm. You stood in what was right and what was fair. "

George Brown, left, and Ricky Archer praised the Wreck Bay community for its work to gain sole management. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

He encouraged those gathered to look to the lessons of the past in moving forward to sole management.

"Today's event has never been about land — it was and continues to be about identity, self-determination and the passing of knowledge from one generation to the next," he said.

" Let's keep putting culture, country and our community first in every decision we make together as we transition to sole management. "

Director of Parks Australia, Djungan man Ricky Archer, told the crowd the department will support the community's plans.

"I just wanted to reiterate Parks Australia's strong support towards working with the Wreck Bay Aboriginal Community Council to realising what sole management looks like and developing a strong pathway to get there," he said.

Stories from Wreck Bay

Doonooch Dancers performed a smoking ceremony and cultural dance at the ceremony grounds in Jervis Bay. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

The 30th anniversary celebrations saw Green Patch come alive with a smoking ceremony and traditional performances from the Doonooch Dancers, choir performances and live music.

A new book of oral histories called Bugiya, Nhaway, Buraadja, which means yesterday, today and tomorrow, was also launched at the celebrations.

"What better time than today to launch it on the 30th anniversary of the hand back of country, so the handing back of country are these stories as well," Kain Ardl, cultural heritage officer for Wreck Bay Aboriginal Community Council, said.

At the celebrations, Aunty Annette Brown presented the Welcome to Country. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

Mr Ardl said the book began as an oral history project with the community.

"There are personal stories in there from elders and children and it's our first ever book that we've written in the community. We're hoping for more to come after this edition," he said.

Kain Ardl and Paul Collis launched new book Bugiya, Nhaway, Buraadja at Booderee's 30th handback anniversary. (ABC Illawarra: Sarah Moss)

Paul Collis teaches Indigenous Studies at the University of Canberra and was approached by the Wreck Bay community to help with the book three years ago.

Dr Collis said it was a joyful project.

"There's been sadness, there's been loss, there's been great joy and discovery as well and I think the book is an amazing amalgam of different voices of this community in a very historic moment," he said.

title: Day rues missed opportunities after golden chance at the Masters
text:
Jason Day was unable to disguise his anguish after blowing his final-round chances to finish a bittersweet joint-12th at the Masters.

Australia's only weekend contender could not buy a putt in a deflating, birdie-free three-over-par round of 75 at Augusta National on Monday morning (AEST).

Day was tied for fifth and just three shots behind after the third round, believing he had a chance to finally claim an elusive green jacket 15 years after finishing runner-up on debut.

"Actually played OK. Just didn't take advantage of [holes] two and three," Day said.

"You get birdies there. That obviously changes the storyline a little bit."

A wretched double-bogey on the par-3 seventh realistically cruelled Day's chances of featuring down the stretch.

Jason Day hits out of the bunker on the 18th hole. (Getty Images: Jared C. Tilton)

"Actually hit 13 greens and played pretty good," he said.

"Just didn't capitalise on any opportunities. Putted quite poorly today.

"So a little unfortunate, but it's just how it goes. I mean, I'm pretty positive about my game."

Day's silver lining is an invitation back next year after the former world number one made a huge sand save for par on the last to finish a respectable equal-12th alongside five-time major winner Brooks Koepka and past champions Jordan Spieth, Patrick Reed and Hideki Matsuyama.

"Obviously it's nice to be able to come back next year but a little disappointing," he said.

"I know I can play well around here. It's just obviously when you get the opportunities, just take them.

"Sometimes you have to take them. Sometimes you have to take some unnecessary risk and sometimes you've got to take a step back and play the way you need to play.

"Unfortunately, I just didn't play the way that I needed to."

Compounding Day's frustration was seeing his English playing partner, Justin Rose, tie for third after starting the day on the same score and grabbing a two-shot lead at one point early on the back nine.

"I was watching Rosey on the front side and he got off to a dream start," Day said.

"That's kind of in my mind where I saw a lot of birdies — minus five — what he did.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"But a lot of the birdies that he made I saw those and just didn't get myself in the position."

Adam Scott, the 2013 champion and only other Australian to make the halfway cut, also cut a frustrated figure after sharing 24th spot at two under after a final-round 70.

"I just never really got it going. I don't know, overall, I guess I blame the putter," he said.

"I just didn't get it rolling on the greens and take advantage of some of the good golf and get any real momentum going.

"The last three holes on Friday really shut me out of a chance yesterday to get back in it like everyone else got back in it."

Next month's PGA Championship will be Scott's 99th consecutive major as he closes in on an incredible milestone that no other active player can boast.

title: 'Incredible man': Athletes mourn pioneering wheelchair racer Bob Hall
text:
Bob Hall, a childhood polio survivor who became known as the father of wheelchair racing after twice winning the Boston Marathon and then went on to build racing chairs for generations of competitors, has died aged 74.

The Boston Athletic Association said on Monday morning (AEST) that Hall's family had confirmed his death after a long illness.

In 1975, Hall convinced Boston Marathon organisers to let him compete in the race and was promised the same finishers' certificate received by runners if he completed the distance in under three hours. In 1970, Vietnam War veteran Eugene Roberts, who had lost both of his legs in the war, needed more than six hours to finish.

Hall crossed the line in 2:58.

"It had nothing to do with, per se, the marathon, but it was about the inclusion," Hall said last year, when he served as the grand marshal in Boston on the 50th anniversary of his pioneering ride.

"It was that I was bringing people along."

Hall crosses the Queensboro Bridge into Manhattan during the New York City Marathon in 1977. (Getty Images)

Hall returned to the Boston race in 1977, when it was designated as the site for the National Wheelchair Championship, and prevailed in a field of seven. As they crested Heartbreak Hill, eventual men's winner Bill Rodgers and fifth-place finisher Tom Fleming slowed to encourage him.

"The interaction was a sign that we were fully accepted as athletes," Hall said.

Hall, who lost the use of both legs from childhood polio, sued in 1978 to have wheelchair racers admitted into the New York Marathon, a fight that wasn't settled until the race created men's and women's wheelchair divisions in 2000.

Hall checks out a wheelchair that was set to be used in the Boston Marathon in 1995. (Getty Images: David L. Ryan)

"Bob Hall is an incredible man," five-time Boston winner and eight-time Paralympic gold medallist Tatyana McFadden said last year.

"I'm so thankful for him. And I think we all are, as wheelchair racers, because he really paved the way."

Hall finished in the top three in Boston on three other occasions and remained active with the race. More than 1,900 wheelchair racers have followed him from Hopkinton to Boston.

This year's race on April 20 will include 50 more, along with 50 others in eight para divisions competing for more than $300,000 in prize money.

The BAA said Hall taught "how we can continue to ensure athletes of all abilities have competitive opportunities on the highest stage here in Boston".

"Bob designed innovative wheelchair equipment, raced with courage, and was proud to be a two-time Boston Marathon champion," the BAA said.

Bob Hall speaks with interacts with race officials before the Boston 5K last year. (Getty Images: Erica Denhoff/Icon Sportswire)

"He helped lead a technological change, transforming simple wheelchairs into racing chairs built for peak athletic performance. Bob's influence and effort five decades ago led to the global circuit of wheelchair racing today."

Many of the competitors — including McFadden and seven-time Boston winner Marcel Hug — learned to race in chairs built by Hall.

"Because of him crossing that finish line, we're able to race today. And it's evolved so much since then," McFadden said last year.

"It was him. It was him being brave and saying, 'I'm going to go out and do this because I believe that we should be able to race Boston Marathon just like everyone else.' So he had the courage to do that."

title: Tour operator cops $330K in cancellations as cyclone relief arrives for businesses
text:
Perched on the edge of the Indian Ocean, early April mornings in Exmouth usually hum with activity.

Minibuses drive tourists to the region's famous snorkelling trips, cafes open at dawn, and even the skies buzz with spotter planes scanning the nearby Ningaloo Reef.

But a fortnight after ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle struck the town more than 1,200 kilometres north of Perth, its streets are quiet.

"Town would normally be absolutely heaving now, without a car park anywhere," restaurant owner Jodie Moir said.

" Instead, the car parks are empty. "

Her partner, Adam Moir, who also operated two whale shark tour boats, said they had lost $330,000 in bookings since the cyclone hit as a category four system.

"If this drags on for another six months, things will get very dire," he said.

"We're not going to be able to replace a motor, for example, which might mean we run into issues next year with the boat not being reliable."

Ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle cut off Exmouth from visitors for weeks. (Supplied: Karley Lillycrapp)

Relief package 'a small difference'

Yesterday, the WA government set aside $1.45 million in funds to help businesses affected by the cyclone.

One-off payments of $10,000 may be claimed by employers with fewer than four workers, while up to $20,000 is available to those with four or more.

Vouchers are also on offer to tourists for 50 per cent off select tours and experiences along the Coral Coast, which stretches north from Cervantes to Exmouth.

It adds to the premier's emergency support scheme, announced late last month.

Some cyclone-hit businesses have been forced to let go of their staff. (ABC News: Bridget McArthur)

Mr Moir said the additional assistance made a "small difference", but the real problem was bed shortages.

"That's where the bottleneck is at the moment," he said.

"If the state government could fund a team of electricians to get up here and get power to the sites, that would definitely allow the caravan parks to open sooner."

Exmouth's airport reopened last week on a reduced schedule after sustaining heavy damage. (ABC Pilbara: Alistair Bates)

Ms Moir said the knock-on effects were driving working holiday-makers away from the town.

She was recently forced to let seven of her staff go before they could start their first shift.

"My workforce is 95 per cent backpackers," Ms Moir said.

"If they can't get their 88 days (of regional work), they're going to lose a whole year off their visa.

"Losing four weeks of work is an absolutely huge hit for them."

Minister promises more support

Travelling to Exmouth on the day of the announcement, WA Tourism Minister Reece Whitby said more accommodation was coming online.

"There is accommodation available in the towns at Coral Bay, Exmouth and Carnarvon," he said.

"And we'll do all we can to help those other businesses that have been severely impacted [and] are looking at a longer delay."

Mr Whitby said he would consider lobbying the federal Department of Home Affairs to change visa requirements for affected backpackers.

"If there are issues I need to take up with the federal government in terms of visa requirements, I'll do that as well," he said.

Evan Hall welcomed the influx of cash for affected tour operators. (ABC News: Lauren Smith)

Tourism Council WA chief executive Evan Hall threw his support behind special allowances for visa holders.

"We want to make sure that they're eligible for their second and third visa … that they're able to stay in those towns," he said.

"They're absolutely crucial for our recovery."

title: Handwritten church music 'caked in dust' performed after 50 years
text:
In historians' terms, Sam Doering realised he had opened a treasure chest.

Last May, he was rummaging inside Point Pass Immanuel Lutheran Church, researching a book marking the church's 150th anniversary.

In the Sunday school building, where Adelaide's Immanuel College began, he found a chest more than half a metre wide "caked in dust".

Sam Doering at Point Pass Immanuel Lutheran Church with the chest of rediscovered sheet music. (ABC North & West SA: George Yankovich)

"We flipped the lid and discovered a trove of music material — most of it German, a large amount of it handwritten," he said.

" I just couldn't believe it. This sort of stuff usually just doesn't survive. "

First performance in at least 50 years, historian says

The browned paper bore names of parishioners from nearby St Peter's Church, which split from Point Pass Immanuel Lutheran Church in 1885 and reunited in 1960.

Many were hymns familiar to the German diaspora, who settled the area around Eudunda during the 1860s.

Sam Doering says more work is needed to properly date the music. (ABC North & West SA: George Yankovich)

Much of the sheet music bears the names of parishioners and students. (ABC North & West SA: George Yankovich)

On Saturday, the Tanunda Liedertafel choir performed two of those hymns — I Worship the Power of Love and O Sacred Head, Now Wounded — along with other chorales.

Mr Doering, who descended from German migrants, said the settlers "had a strong choral tradition and maintained that from about the 1920s to the 1950s".

The church's distinctive golden spire was built in 1912 and refurbished this year. (Supplied: Connor Chidgey)

"There is a lot of work to be done to investigate the provenance of this music, but certainly it is religious in nature," he said.

"There are names attached of the members that kept this choir going … a few of the congregation knew that the box was there, but it was basically gathering dust for probably 50 years.

"We're not sure how it made its way from St Peter's to Immanuel, but thank goodness it did."

'Connection from past to present'

Liedertafel conductor Matthew Falland said the music was "lovingly translated for the choir" of the day.

"The music was in small booklets, and it was all handwritten, the notes and words," he said.

"Whoever it was had a very clear hand and it was simple enough to find the lyrics online, and check it against the copy."

Tanunda Liedertafel choir conductor Matthew Falland (second from right) with members of the choir. (ABC North & West SA: George Yankovich)

Both pieces were familiar to him and he said it was "lovely to hear they were part of the church repertoire".

" There's a joy to hearing music that's seldom sung. It's that connection from past to present that still exists here. "

Apart from the music, another story about the church's history reflects broader religious currents.

In researching his book, Mr Doering came across German newspapers reporting on an episode of the wider 1885 Australian Lutheran split.

The original Immanuel College in 1914, with the church visible in background. (Supplied: Lutheran Archives)

"There were a number of dissenting members who decided to break into the church on multiple occasions," he said.

"On one occasion, there were at least two gentlemen who broke through the front doors, and then decided to have a rest.

"When midnight came, they broke into the main part of the church and got the fright of their lives.

"The pastor was standing by the altar in full vestments, the congregation gathered, the organ started playing, and these two gentlemen were terrified.

"They remained quiet until the service ended at about 3am and went home … thankfully, saner minds prevailed and there was a resolution shortly after."

Congregation looks to the future

Leading up to Saturday, the church's golden spire, built in 1912, was removed via crane and the wooden frame refurbished.

The Point Pass Immanuel Lutheran Church spire was removed for refurbishment earlier this year. (Supplied: Connor Chidgey)

Point Pass had a population of 123 at the last census, but more than 200 guests turned up on the weekend.

Among them was Gordon Schutz, 101, who taught there for more than three decades as a Sunday school teacher.

His ancestors were also founding congregation members.

Asked whether the church had changed much, he said: "Oh, yes."

"In my younger days, the men sat on one side and the ladies on the other," Mr Schutz said.

"It was all in German in those days. I was confirmed in German, and there were 14 of us on … 18 September, 1938, but I'm the only one still living."

Gordon Schutz, 101, at Point Pass Immanuel Lutheran Church for its 150th anniversary. (ABC North & West SA: George Yankovich)

Gordon Schutz (front left) in an undated photo, posing with students from his Sunday school class. (Supplied: Samuel Doering)

Church elder Bruce Schutz, a relative, said there were about 40 regular churchgoers.

"It's smaller than it was, but we've got a lot of young ones at the moment, so it's still got a little while yet," he said.

"It gives me confidence. Young people are our future and, without them, there's no church."

As for a 300th anniversary, he said the building was "still in good shape".

"It's been fixed up pretty well. The salt damp has been dealt with; the spire was redone 35 years ago before this time around, so there's no reason that it can't be."

title: Mystery of seventh man unsolved, months after Chinese boat arrivals
text:
With a population of only about 300 people, unexpected visitors are conspicuous in one of Australia's most isolated communities.

In December, Matilda Djanghara quickly noticed two strangers during a regular evening stroll in Kalumburu, 3,000 kilometres north of Perth.

"One was tall, one was short, one had well-dressed clothes on, he said, 'Hello, good evening,'" she said.

Matilda Djanghara says she ran into two of the Chinese men while walking around the community. (ABC Kimberley: Giulia Bertoglio)

It was a quick exchange, and while it raised questions, she did not think too much of it until the next day.

"Then I heard coast guards came along the next morning, and said they were looking for five or six Indonesians," she said.

Australian Border Force (ABF) later confirmed it had detained six Chinese nationals who had reached Kalumburu via boat in early December.

News of their arrival quickly made national headlines, triggering fierce political debate about the protection of national borders.

Men who arrived at the remote WA community of Kalumburu are escorted onto a boat by border force officials in December. (Supplied)

For Kalumburu residents, it fuelled rumours, fear and even humour.

Ms Djanghara laughed while explaining she heard the men had asked for directions to McDonald's. The closest outlet is a 12-hour drive away in Broome.

"[They] came to the wrong place; this is a community," she said.

But questions still linger about a seventh man who residents say tried to enter the community on a closed road at the same time.

The seventh man

Resident Uriah Waina said he helped an unexpected visitor who was bogged on the only access road, which was closed due to wet-season flooding.

"He was at the crossing, the Carson River Crossing, maybe 10 kilometres on the other side, and he was bogged," he said.

Uriah Waina says he helped unbog a vehicle that was attempting to enter Kalumburu during the wet season. (ABC Kimberley: Giulia Bertoglio)

Mr Waina described him as a "big" and "solid" Chinese man.

"We asked him what he was doing and he said he was coming to pick up the guys that came off the boat," he said.

The man stayed in Kalumburu for a night while his car was being retrieved, Mr Waina said.

"We brought him in, and they put him in the Coconut Lodge for one night, then the next day we helped him get his car out of the bog, then he turned around and drove back to Broome," he said.

Mr Waina said he was surprised the man was able to leave.

"I just know I took him out of the bog and sent him on his way," he said.

The ABC understands the man hired a rental car at Broome Airport and returned it a couple of days later.

Kalumburu Aboriginal Corporation (KAC) chief executive Kim Holm said the man did not have a permit to travel on the closed road and to enter Kalumburu, which is a closed community and requires all guests to obtain a visitor's permit.

Ms Holm said the man was found on Kalumburu Road and locals spent a long time helping him.

"It's just not safe, and it's not passable," she said.

Kim Holm says the unexpected arrival of the men made some residents in the community fearful. (ABC Kimberley: Giulia Bertoglio)

"It took a lot of hours and manpower to get him unbogged and on his way.

" The community were … certainly suspicious he was connected to the Chinese that came in but I've received no confirmation on that. "

It is unknown whether the man was arrested or charged, with the ABF and Australian Federal Police declining to comment.

People-smuggling changes

It is not the first time a group of Chinese nationals has arrived in the Kimberley by boat, with another group entering the Truscott-Mungalalu Airbase in 2024.

Recent reports suggest Australia is becoming analternative destination to the United States in global people-smuggling routes involving Chinese nationals, thanks to the Trump administration's border crackdown.

In January, Indonesian media reported authorities had dismantled an international people-smuggling syndicate, which had sent Chinese nationals to Australia via Indonesia.

Different Indigenous languages are spoken in WA's northernmost community, Kalumburu. (ABC Kimberley: Dunja Karagic)

Antje Missbach, a professor of sociology at Germany's Bielefeld University who has specialised in people smuggling in Indonesia for more than a decade, said while numbers were hard to determine, there had been a shift in who was attempting to enter Australia.

"The people who are now arriving in Australia are Chinese and probably most people would not call them asylum seekers in the first place … but rather people are trying for better economic, financial and ways of life," Professor Missbach said.

She said rather than attempting to reach Australian outposts like Ashmore Reef or Christmas Island, there were more efforts to enter the Australian mainland undetected.

"People are trying to sneak into Australia, not because they want to apply for protection but rather they want to establish a new life, by presumably living under the radar," she said.

When arriving in Australia, Professor Missbach said it was common to have someone who spoke the language of those arriving to assist on the ground, especially in a remote area like Kalumburu.

"To enter people into a country secretly, you do need to organise a pick-up. There is no way people could get public transport or just make their way to a big city on their own," Professor Missbach said.

"If he had been at the right point in time, if his car hadn't broken down, he might have just picked up the six people and taken them and disappeared, nobody would have noticed.

" Often we find out about these operations because there is some failure involved. "

Meanwhile, for residents living in Kalumburu, Mr Waina said the latest arrival had left him feeling a "bit nervous".

Kalumburu has one clinic, one store and limited accommodation, and Ms Holm said it was not equipped to deal with unplanned visits.

"Our clinic gets put under extra pressure. We only have two police to man the whole community, so that takes them offline from all the other community issues if they're dealing with something like that," she said.

While Ms Holm said the incident had triggered some funny rumours in the community, there were some genuine concerns.

"Because we're such a remote community, you know who is coming, who is going; when people turn up unexpectedly, it's not normal."

title: Live: ASX slips as oil surges back above $US100 on US threat of Gulf blockade
text:
Oil prices jumped above $US100/barrel on Monday as the US Navy prepared a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz that could restrict Iranian oil shipments after the US and Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war.

Brent crude futures rose $7.60, or 8%, to $102.80 a barrel after settling 0.8% lower on Friday.

US West Texas Intermediate was at $104.88 a barrel, up $8.31, or 8.6%, following a 1.3% loss in the previous session.

"The market is now largely back to conditions before the ceasefire, except now the US will block the remaining up to 2 million barrels per day Iranian-linked flows through the Strait of Hormuz as well," head of energy research at MST Marquee Saul Kavonic told Reuters.

President Donald Trump said on Sunday the US Navy would start blockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war, jeopardising a fragile two-week ceasefire.

He added that the price of oil and gasoline may remain high through November's midterm elections, a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from his decision to attack Iran six weeks ago.

US Central Command said U.S. forces would begin implementing the blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports at 10 am ET (midnight AEST) on Monday.

"Not only does this restrain exports from Persian Gulf oil producers, but it will also restrict Iran's ability to export oil and will exacerbate the supply disruptions the market is experiencing," ANZ analysts Brian Martin and Daniel Hynes said in a note.

IG market analyst Tony Sycamore said the move would effectively choke off the flow of Iranian oil, forcing Tehran's allies and customers to apply the necessary pressure to get the waterway reopened.

Iran's Revolutionary Guards said on Sunday that any military vessels attempting to approach the Strait of Hormuz would be considered a violation of the two-week U.S. ceasefire and be dealt with harshly and decisively.

Despite the stalemate, three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed.

They appeared to be the first vessels to exit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal was struck last week.

No other ships were spotted in the strait on Monday except for one Iran-flagged vessel anchored there, shipping data on LSEG showed.

On Sunday, Saudi Arabia said it has restored full oil pumping capacity through the East-West pipeline to about 7 million barrels per day, days after providing an assessment of damage to its energy sector from attacks during the Iran conflict.

Reporting by Reuters

title: Live: Australia not asked to help Hormuz blockade as PM names new defence chief
text:
Shadow NDIS minister Melissa McIntosh was in Penrith a bit earlier, talking about the need for an overhaul of the National Disability Insurance Scheme.

The government has been floating reforms to the scheme ahead of the federal budget, but there's been some mixed messaging from Labor MPs about what's on the table.

Last week, NDIS minister Mark Butler said he was open to the idea of narrowing eligibility to the scheme via means testing, but the door has since been closed by treasurer Jim Chalmers and the prime minister.

McIntosh says she's received an "overwhelming" number of calls and emails from people worried about the future of their plans.

"Every time the government floats a new idea around the NDIS it causes distress amongst those Australians who rely on the NDIS".

She says the government needs to be focusing on stamping out the rorts within the scheme, including mandating registrations for service providers.

"It's really mind blowing to think you don't need any qualifications, you don't need to have any basic safety standards to be an NDIS provider looking after our most vulnerable".

title: US-Iran war LIVE updates: Trump says US will ‘blockade’ the Strait of Hormuz after peace talks failed over Iran’s ‘nuclear ambitions’
text:
Donald Trump’s plan to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has had a major impact on commodity and equity markets this morning.

Crude oil prices alone have jumped by more than 7 per cent in early trade. West Texas finished last week’s trade around $US95 a barrel, but this morning, investors have pushed it back to $US104 a barrel.

It’s a similar story for Brent crude, which has also climbed in price by more than 7 per cent.

The Australian dollar, which was approaching US71¢ late last week, dipped below US70¢ this morning, before recovering a little to sit around US70.19¢.

The ASX200 opened down by 0.4 per cent given the uncertainty over how long the current ceasefire will hold.

Unsurprisingly, share prices in energy companies have climbed, including Karoon Energy (up 6.2 per cent), Viva Energy (up 4.8 per cent) and Woodside (up 3.1 per cent).

Viking Mines has switched on a fresh US trading line for its shares, with the company now officially quoted on the OTC Markets under the ticker VKALF. OTC or “over-the-counter” is a US trading platform that allows broker-dealers to match buyers and sellers outside the big exchanges such as the New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq. Viking Mines has begun trading on the US OTC Markets, giving North American investors a local-currency gateway to its Nevada-based Linka tungsten project. For local punters, it is business as usual with Viking’s primary listing staying put on the ASX under VKA and importantly, the company says it has not issued any new shares as part of the move. The new quotation will make life easier for North American retail and institutional traders looking to get exposure to Viking’s Nevada-based Linka tungsten project.

Advertisement

‘We look forward to commencing US investor engagement to grow the Company’s profile and attract new shareholders’. Viking Mines managing director and chief executive officer Julian Woodcock For Viking, it can mean greater visibility, improved accessibility and potentially stronger liquidity, as a wider pool of tungsten followers back the stock in their local currency and time zone. The new market access doesn’t appear entirely coincidental. Just 11 days ago, Viking outlined an accelerated Linka workflow to rapidly push the project down the value chain. The company’s roadmap includes a stack of parallel workstreams covering metallurgy, geophysics and three-dimensional modelling, along with early-stage studies that feature advanced ore sorting and processing technologies. Viking is also taking a closer look at mineralised historical stockpiles and tailings material as potential low-hanging fruit for a quick restart. Viking Mines managing director and chief executive officer Julian Woodcock said: “Listing on the OTC Markets provides exposure to the substantial US investment market and naturally complements our tungsten activities in Nevada. While our primary listing remains on the ASX, releasing all announcements on the OTC platform allows us to build recognition as a critical mineral developer while tungsten remains highly valuable.”

Advertisement

On the ground, Viking is eyeing up a June-quarter drilling campaign, with a Notice of Intent to be lodged with the US Bureau of Land Management soon. The company is also investigating opportunities for cleaner flotation results on the near-term radar, as it works to optimise recovery while maintaining marketable concentrate grades. While the expedited workflow is seen as critical to generating cash flows sooner rather than later, Viking is also leaning into the bigger long-term picture. With tungsten prices continuing to surge and ammonium paratungstate quoted at US$3,150 (A$4480) per metric tonne unit, the company is pitching Linka as a strategically placed critical minerals play in a Tier-1 mining jurisdiction. Management says the OTC listing broadens its global shareholder base by removing barriers for US-based punters, while keeping disclosure standards aligned with its ASX reporting. With a US trading presence now in place and a steady stream of technical milestones in the pipeline, Viking looks set for an active quarter as it gears up to unleash the rigs at Linka and pull its first real answers straight from the drill bit.
